But also, maybe before we kind of like jump in, I know this is just kind of like getting to know each other a little bit, but I would love to hear a little bit about your journey because it's pretty unique, you know?
Yeah, yeah, I've taken some left and right steps, that's for sure.
Well, where would, so, yeah, I guess I can start before TechCrunch and then get into TechCrunch and then go after TechCrunch.
Yeah.
That's probably a good worldview.
Before TechCrunch, you know, I...
I was kind of scrappy and didn't really know what I was doing, to be perfectly honest with you.

I'm a classically trained pianist, and I was really just trying to make ends meet while I had gotten into making electronic music and playing, I'll just say it, playing raves and stuff like that with my buddies.
And I was sort of filling the gaps with various full-time work, like working at Pebble Beach down in Monterey and stuff like that.
And then I got into producing events.
As you can imagine, that made a lot of sense because I was running around to Burning Man and doing all this stuff myself.
And I also learned how to make electronic music in college.
I have a piano performance degree and all that sort of stuff.
And so I got into producing events.
so
And was pretty hardcore involved in that for, I don't know, seven or eight years.
And then I went, this guy named Ron Johnson hired me to work at Mosaic Event Management.
And I started sort of just doing full on, I guess, experience marketing for people is the best way to describe it.
And I did that.
I took over.
TechCrunch came to Mosaic and said, hey, we want you guys to run Disrupt for us.
And I said, and we said yes.
And I was the person that was running Disrupt at a production level for Mosaic.
And that led me to meet the COO of TechCrunch, Ned Desmond, who came to me and said, hey, look, I know you're working for this company.
We really like you a whole lot.
Why don't you just come on board and take over all the event stuff for TechCrunch?
And I loved TechCrunch, you know.
I've always been tech savvy and really interested.
And I said, sure.
And I said, with one caveat, I just got into grad school and I'm going to get my MBA.
And he said, great.
You can do both things at once.
And I was like, what?
Unstressful at all, an MBA and running events.
Yeah.
And then we also welcomed our second child, my wife and I, into our lives.
You know, we have a 23-month-old, so.
Yeah.
So the first couple of years for TechCrunch, I was running the event piece.
And we launched our international event operation during that time.
So once a month, I was flying to Europe for about a week or week and a half.
And then I would come back and work on my MBA.
And every Wednesday, I would fly up to San Francisco and be at my desk from 9 a.m.
to 6 p.m.
And then fly back down to San Diego.
And then after I got...
I got done with that interesting era of my life at my MBA.
You know, you could imagine a lot of people, there's a lot of things I could have done.
And Ned said to me, why don't you just take over the entire business side of TechCrunch, you know, run the P&L, run our ad operations, run, you know, all these things.
And I said, sure.
And took that on, moved back to San Francisco.
And then, you know, over the years after, so I got done with that in like 2014, I guess.
And I just sort of rapidly learned the entire publishing businesses.
And then Ned left and I took over his job and I was the GM of TechCrunch for a years.
That's kind of my story in a heartbeat.
I don't know if it all made sense, but that's how I got to where I am today.
And now, and then after being the GM of TechCrunch for a few years, Insight Partners contacted me.
I said, hey, we want to run a media roll up.
Will you do it for us?
So I said, yes.
And I came over and they formed this LLC called Insight Media Group.
And they had one media asset at the time, put that under that umbrella.
And I've been rolling up.
I'm in the process of rolling up companies.
I'm buying one or two a year in the publishing space.
So that's that's my journey.
That's really amazing.
Like, oh, my God, I have so many, so many questions.
But the most important one, are you having fun?
Yeah, yeah, I'm having a lot of fun.
You know, like I was able to convince a lot of my well, a lot of my colleagues were interested in joining after I left.
So I have, you know, some of my really important former colleagues around, you know, you know how it is when you're working.
In a business, it's nice to have people that you trust and understand what the outcomes are going to be when you work with them.
So I have a lot of those folks around me.
Insight Partners has been great.
You know, we're a strategic asset.
not like a company that they're trying to run the valuation up and eject from portfolios for some sort of exit reasons, that kind of thing.

But I have access to the PE resources.
Like if I want to buy something, you know, all of a sudden an army of analysts will gather around me and, you know, scrub the balance sheet and do all that sort of stuff.
And then when it comes to funding, I can draw on funding from, you know, various places to get it done.
It's all very quick.
Like, but if – but the company, it's –
Also, I'm buying small to mid-sized companies.

I'm buying publications, newsletters.
Roadmap.sh is a platform.

And they publish these learning platforms.

And then we launched an SQL course.
But it's not just about SQL.

It has like all this AI assistance built into it.

So it can help you, like, you know, essentially you have a feedback mechanism right there that will, you know, tailor itself to whatever you need to know in order to get through the various modules of this thing.
So.
Yeah.
So, yes, I'm having a lot of fun is the answer to your question.
That sounds pretty cool.
Well, happy to give you some context.

don't know how much context Matthew gave you, but essentially, like, my entire career, was at different enterprises and companies like IBM and HP and always kind of convincing them to, hey, we should be more like a publisher.
We should act like a media company.
I was always the person, like, coaching journalists to come over the dark side of marketing and produce actual good content and, you know, and build trust and scale that up.
And then it turned out it became a thing called content marketing after a while.
And then, you know, but I would always just be like hire people from like Wired and Computer World and be like, hey, all right, I need you to write about DevOps now and do a publication.
And recruit some really good contributors.
And then scaled us up.
then when I was at HP, I built a site called techbeacon.com.
Unfortunately, it sunset a few years back, but we ended up recruiting almost a thousand contributors.

So it was like kind of a contributor network, highly editorialized with four managing editors, producing like 20 articles a week, but a very more technical, and grew to about a million visitors a month.
Nothing crazy, but it was pretty good for being owned by a company like HP.
I did really well.
then throughout my career, I just kept doing that, you know.

And so essentially, like I then was hired with big corporate and went to work for different startups, got lucky enough in some ways to work for a lot of them that did well, like HashiCorp went public, Service Titan more recently went public, Skill.ai did decent, and despite being super toxic to work for, you know.
And, you you know.

And then more recently, was at this company called Deepgram, and I just started taking everything I had learned about, like, how to help produce good content that drove growth for companies, you know, and started building these AI workflows to scale that up, right?
And I helped Deepgram 24-hour traffic.
We went from about 1,000 to about 30,000 people a day visiting the site.
I built this AI apps catalog that was kind of like a product hunt, but for only AI apps.
I built this newsletter called aiminds.com.

I grew to 300,000 subscribers, and then I launched a glossary, started ramping up, you know, the blog content and, you know, like all of that, building, like, workflows and editors in the loop, essentially, you know, and writers in the loop.
And so I was like, oh, this is cool.
So then a lot of people started asking me, hey, why don't you start doing workshops teaching people what you did?
It's pretty cool.
And so I did the first one last April, and then we had, like, 30 people paid, like, 500 bucks to come to it.
And then I was just like teaching people.
By the end of it, a couple of them were like, hey, it's really cool.
I just don't have the time.
I have a full-time job.
Can you just build the workflows for me, hire the editors in the loop, but then help with the strategy a little bit?
And I was like, okay, yeah, sure.
So I'd said yes to two.
And then fast forward, we did a few more workshops, and then it just started to kind of snowball.
And then around September of last year, I partnered with my CTO co-founder, Daniel, who had a product and engineering at a company called IFTTT.
I don't know if you remember if this and that.
Back in the day, it was like the precursor to Zapier workflow automation, turning on your lights or whatever, a lot of the consumer stuff.
And then we just started turning a lot of these workflows and scaling them up.
And what we started to realize, like, we needed to build an engine to build workflows, as opposed to just manually build these workflows and prompt it, right?
And so we sent...
So we built an AI agent that builds workflows and then a runtime layer and observability layer to run all of that.
can show you too.
And then started hiring the editors in the loop and the strategists.
And then things kind of snowballed.
And we now have, I think, 45 customers and getting close to 70 in ARR profitably.
And then three, four weeks ago, we raised our Series A.
We went straight to A because we were profitable.
And yeah, and then it's been kind of nuts.
know, it's just selling.

yeah, yeah.

Yeah, like the way it kind of works is, you know, I think your publications might be.
In a different territory, right?

But most companies, not publications, they don't have the ability to go and hire a head of content, a managing editor, a few writers, production assistant, a graphic designer, to then start bringing out content.
And then six months in, start to optimize that and then figure out what's happening, right?
And so because of that, most companies default to hiring an agency.
Usually, and the problem with agencies and content agencies is that they need to make a high margin and it's a really bad business.
So then they end up hiring really bad writers and they don't have the in-house expertise and they don't have a lot of tools and automation and engineers working for them, right?
And so because of that, they're doing, you know, charging one, two, two dollars a word and not giving a lot of strategy.
Or if you hire an SEO agency, they're giving you, hey, here's what you need to fix.
Good luck.
luck.

Essentially, what we're doing is the entire team as a service, and the reason we're able to deliver the whole entire team as a service and deliver 50 to 100,000 words plus a month is because AI is doing so much of the lag work, and we're taking so much of the expertise and making them into these workflows, right?

But then it feels like a white glove service because we're meeting every week, we're coming up with a strategy, we're helping them scale content the way they want to do it, we're helping to scale programmatic pages and things like that.
And so essentially, it's not quite a license to platform, although we're getting a lot of requests to license our tech under the hood a little bit.
But I don't know if that makes sense a little bit.
Oh, it makes perfect sense to me.
I mean, I'm looking at all kinds of these solutions from a publisher level.
Like, I don't know if you've seen this group, societies.io, where they basically, what they will do is they can look at a network.
quick.

of users that you have, understand, you know, how they've engaged and create a synthetic version of that user and network users, like an entire audience.
And then you can test messages, like you can type up a message, send it out to the audience, see how the audience, that synthetic audience interacts.
But then it automatically creates 100 iterations of your message and tests it against your synthetic audience and comes up with the version of it that performs the best.
The public version of it only allows you to do this with LinkedIn, but they, yeah, so they can, so that's just one, I think that's probably one version of what you're doing.

Like, it sounds like you're, you have artificial intelligence that's optimizing, you know, SEO, like keywords and metadata and probably doing all kinds of, and then just also the content itself is like.

I love demos.

Okay, so, so essentially, like, just to kind of show you a little bit under the hood, just to produce, like, let's say one piece of content, these, like, the representation of some of our workflows, and obviously, like, each one of these boxes can be, like, multiple steps and iterative and, you know, but, but it's like, and each company can be different, right?
There might be more or less or different steps.
But, you know, like, what do you do?
Like, you identify opportunities, right?
Topics you want to create, and then you go and do a bunch of research on them.
Okay, so we created a research.
one that I'll show you in a second.
It's like, okay, now you have all this research, you're stored in a vector database, you're retrieving it, and now you want to come up with a plan.

But you want to come up with a plan, not in a vacuum, you want to come up with a plan with the context of the entire research and the context of what your company is or your publication is, and the context of what persona you're targeting for that specific piece, right?
And the context of everything else, right?
Then after you come up with a plan, and by the way, like, you want an editor to review some of the stuff, right?
At the end or at the beginning, you want to review, like, maybe, like, at certain levels and places, right?
And each customer might be completely different.
Then you want to come up with, like, the angle of the article and whatever.

And then, okay, now you want to start executing against that plan, but you don't want to just execute it in a blank, you want to go and retrieve your knowledge base, and then go do further research in each section to make sure the initial brief didn't miss anything, now that you have the full plan detailed out, right?
And then you want to start stylizing that based on your, like, how you like to style content, based on the archetypes of
Develop for you, the personas, the voice, all that, right?

And then you start, like, the post-processing of, like, hey, adding CTAs, cross-linking, like, fact-checking, you know, optimizing for your primary, secondary keywords, whatever else you want to optimize for.
And then there's, like, image generation, the illustrations, the graphics, all of that.
And then there's, like, all the last mile things of, like, okay, we still need someone to copy and paste this and stage it and check this, this, this, and this manually.
Like, you know, that we also do.
But the cool thing with this is, like, so, like, just to give you an idea, like, let me see the deep researcher, for example.
This one is for school AI.
They're actually, like, in AI tutoring as well to help teachers.
This one is understanding student types in class.
And so it started to generate questions, research questions, right?
So what are the characteristics of the 12 distinct student types?
How are you going to identify, accurately assess different student types?
What are the best practices and strategies to engage them?
How do they understand and address, blah, blah, blah.
What are the roles AI can play in it, right?
Like, and then it's going to start doing like research on each one of these things, you know, each one of these questions and trying to answer.
And then it's going to combine that research in addition to storing to a vector DB and then take it on to the next one, right?
And then all of this is running as code and it's running in our runtime layer, right?
So we have all the granularity, all this data, and we're starting to collect also the intervention data from all the editors, you know, so that that data is super valuable down the line, right?
So you can see like all these workflows running right now, you know, this one is the draft one, right?
And you can see like everything that's happening right now, you know, and it's like generating all of these things.
And if it fails, it can retry multiple times, it can run for minutes, you know?
And then there's literally like descriptions of all the steps as well.
And so like, we're not ready to expose this fully, but everything represented in code, which is kind of nice because like you can run code.
And then as the workflows evolve, you can see how it changed and see the impact of the activities that are run.
But also there's a way for experts to collaborate with all of this, both in architecting the workflows, a.k.a.
coding the workflows, as well as the activities and the outputs and the feedback you can give to these outputs, right?
Yeah.

And then what's really cool, and like we're taking this and like we're generating so much stuff like a call intelligence analysis, researching a topic, you know, we even have things around like hiring and recruiting, you know, like this one is like, taking the role that a person apply, parsing their resume, getting the job requirements, and then assessing the person, you know, based on the answers they gave to different questions, right?
Like this is all happening in parallel.
There we go.
Analyze the resume and then look at the questions, the answers to the questions.
Describe your process for fact-checking and varying firing information.
You ensure accuracy of data within your documents and then like looks at the answer and then analyzes and then at the end makes the final assessment.

And so it's like this is so repurposable, if you will, and that's not really a word, but, you know, and it's really cool because it's all happening in parallel.
So like, you know, the people you would normally hire, except like the people you would normally hire in your publications, right?

It's like they now have this thing supercharging them, but not in a way that's like they have to learn how to be the best prompt engineer in the world, but rather in a way that's just like a sidekick, you know, that's super powerful, you know, that has all that context.
So they don't have to keep like feeding it the context of where they are and the topics and things, you know.
And once they architect how they want to go about doing the work, then this thing can kind of replicate that and supercharge it, you know.
